question.text: Rajiv has a health insurance policy with a compulsory deductible of Rs. 10,000 and a co-pay clause of 20%. If he incurs a hospital bill of Rs. 1,50,000, what amount will the insurance company pay?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The deductible is subtracted first: Rs. 1,50,000 - Rs. 10,000 = Rs. 1,40,000. Then co-pay of 20% is applied on the remainder: 20% of Rs. 1,40,000 = Rs. 28,000 borne by insured. Insurance company pays Rs. 1,40,000 - Rs. 28,000 = Rs. 1,12,000. **The Trap:** Rs. 1,20,000 is obtained if you apply only co-pay (20% of 1,50,000 = 30,000) without deducting the deductible first. Rs. 1,18,000 results from applying co-pay first and then deductible, which is the wrong sequence. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A (Mutual Fund Distributors) and Series X-A also test the distinction between deductible and co-pay in insurance-linked questions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always remember the sequence: Deductible is subtracted FIRST from the total claim, and THEN co-pay percentage is applied on the remaining amount. This order significantly affects the out-of-pocket expense for the insured.
